metadata.summary: Evidence-based requirements engineering that anchors each requirement to business questions, quantified baselines, and success metrics. Replaces opinion-driven prioritization with usage, performance, business, and customer-feedback analytics.

content.title: Data-Driven Requirements Engineering
content.summary: An evidence-based approach to requirements definition that replaces opinion-driven prioritization with data analytics. Each requirement is anchored to a business question, quantified baseline metrics, and a measurable success target. Prioritization uses usage data, performance data, business metrics, and customer feedback rather than stakeholder seniority or gut feel.

why.p: Requirements based on stakeholder opinion are frequently wrong about user needs and business impact; prioritization becomes political; success metrics are defined post-implementation when the team wants to declare victory.

Data-driven engineering anchors every requirement to evidence, establishes the baseline before development, and locks success criteria upfront so measurement cannot be gamed. 95% of enterprise AI projects fail without a solid data strategy underpinning requirements — the same principle applies to all feature work.

list.item: Prioritizing a backlog where features compete for limited capacity and ROI data is available.
Feature validation before investment when analytics (usage, conversion, error rate) can answer the business question.
Post-MVP iteration where product analytics reveal which flows users actually take.
A/B test design where the requirement specifies what hypothesis is being tested and what outcome declares success.
AI/ML feature scoping where business impact measurement (cycle time, CSAT, error rate) must be defined before model training.
Greenfield products with no users and no baseline — hypothesis-driven development (Lean Startup) applies before data exists.
Requirements driven by compliance or legal obligation where the mandate, not ROI, is the reason to build.
One-off internal tooling with a known, small user group where analytics instrumentation cost exceeds value.
When the organization has no analytics tooling and instrumenting is out of scope — gather qualitative evidence instead.

rule.statement: Every requirement must trace back to a business question, then to data sources, then to analysis findings before a requirement statement is written. The sequence is: Business Question → Data Collection → Analysis → Insight → Requirement → Success Metrics.
rule.rationale: Requirements written before data analysis capture stakeholder opinion, not evidence. The evidence-based process delays requirement writing until after the analysis phase, flipping the order from traditional waterfall.

rule.statement: Define success metrics — with baseline values and measurement methods — before development starts, not after. Lock metric definitions and baseline queries in the requirement document.
rule.rationale: Post-implementation metric definition enables Goodhart's Law: when teams define metrics after knowing the result, definitions drift to make the number land.

list.item: Business Question — "Why do 38% of users abandon at the payment step?" (framed with time window, audience, decision it informs).
Data Collection — query warehouse/BI for baseline: bounce rate, support tickets, session replays, interviews.
Analysis — synthesize findings: 52% cite "too many fields", 8% fail validation, 5% session timeout.
Insight — root cause identified: form complexity + insufficient validation errors.
Requirement — simplify payment form to 3 required fields with inline validation.
Success Metrics — baseline drop-off 38%, target 27% (11 point improvement), measured via funnel query over 30 days.

section.name: analytics-competencies-for-bas
section.p: Required competencies: statistical analysis (validate assumptions with data), data visualization (communicate insights), SQL/BI tools (query business data), A/B testing (validate solution options), predictive analytics (forecast feature impact).

list.item: BI dashboards (Tableau, Power BI) for KPI monitoring.
SQL interfaces (Mode, Looker) for custom queries.
No-code analytics (Dataiku, Alteryx) for automated workflows.
NLP query (ThoughtSpot) for natural language exploration.

rule.statement: Prioritize requirements using four metric types in combination: usage data (page views, feature adoption) for high-use feature improvements; performance data (error rates, load times) for bottleneck resolution; business metrics (conversion, revenue) for revenue-impacting features; customer feedback (NPS, support tickets) for high-volume pain points.
rule.rationale: Single-metric prioritization is gameable; combining four independent signal types produces a prioritization that is robust to individual metric drift.

list.item: Usage data (page views, feature adoption rate, session depth) — prioritize improvements to high-use features; deprioritize features nobody uses even if stakeholders believe they are important.
Performance data (error rates, latency p95, funnel drop-off rates) — address bottlenecks that degrade user experience for the largest user segments.
Business metrics (conversion rate, revenue per user, churn rate) — focus on features with direct revenue impact measurable within the evaluation window.
Customer feedback (NPS verbatims, support ticket volume by category, user interviews) — address the pain points mentioned most frequently across all feedback channels.

rule.statement: Measure AI/ML feature impact across four areas: operational efficiency (cycle time, throughput, error rate, rework percentage), experience and growth (CSAT/NPS, conversion rate, retention), financial (cost-to-serve, gross margin, working capital), and risk and compliance (policy violations avoided, audit hours saved).
rule.rationale: Single-area measurement (e.g., only efficiency) misses the full value picture; AI features often have their largest impact in a different area than the one that motivated the investment.

rule.statement: For each metric, define the measurement method, data source, baseline value, and target value before development — never after. Use the same query and the same data source for baseline and post-implementation measurement.
rule.rationale: Changing data sources or query definitions between baseline and measurement invalidates variance calculations; the numerator and denominator must be comparable.

list.item: 91.9% of organizations report measurable value from data and analytics investments.
65% have adopted or are investigating AI for analytics.
Data-driven companies are 23x more likely to acquire customers.
95% of enterprise AI projects fail without a solid data strategy underpinning requirements.

example.title: Checkout flow optimization — requirement derived from analytics, not opinion
example.note: Requirement: REQ-142 — Reduce cart abandonment at payment step
example.code:
Business Question: Why do 38% of users who reach payment step not complete checkout?
Data Sources: | Source | Metric | Value | Confidence | |--------------|-------------------------------|--------------|------------| | PostHog | Payment step drop-off rate | 38% | High | | Zendesk | Payment error support tickets | 120/month | High | | Session replay | Top exit reason at step 4 | "Too many fields" — 52% | Medium |
Analysis Summary: Session replay shows 52% of abandoners stop at the payment form. Support tickets confirm credit card field validation errors as #1 complaint. A/B test on a simplified 2-field form in Q3 showed +11% completion on n=2,400.
Requirement Statement: Simplify the payment entry form to 3 required fields (card number, expiry, CVV) with inline validation and auto-detection of card type.
Success Metrics: | Metric | Baseline | Target | Method | |----------------------|----------|--------|---------------------------| | Payment step drop-off | 38% | 27% | PostHog funnel, same cohort def | | Payment support tickets | 120/mo | 80/mo | Zendesk category filter | | Checkout conversion | 62% | 73% | Revenue analytics, 30-day window |
Validation Plan: - A/B test: 50/50 split, minimum 2,000 users per arm, 14-day run - Success criterion: drop-off delta > 8 percentage points, p < 0.05 - Rollback trigger: conversion drops > 2% from baseline in first 48 hours

example.title: Opinion-based requirement without evidence
example.code: Requirement: Improve the checkout experience Reason: Stakeholders think it's too complicated Success: Users are happier
example.note: No data source, no baseline, no measurable success criterion. Cannot prioritize, cannot validate, cannot measure outcome.

section.name: agentic-workflow-four-pass-pipeline
section.p: Drive data-driven requirements as a four-pass pipeline that turns telemetry into reviewable requirements.
Pass 1: Question pass — a sonnet agent reads the business context and emits {business_question, hypothesis, kpi_owner, expected_direction}; no data yet.

Pass 2: Baseline pass — agents query BI / warehouse / product analytics (BigQuery, Snowflake, Mixpanel, PostHog, GA4) and emit {kpi, current_value, segment, as_of_date, source_url, n}. Parallel agents per data source reduce blocking on slow warehouses.

Pass 3: Requirement pass — given baseline + hypothesis, an opus agent writes the structured requirement (the README template) and the success-metric table; a separate agent runs power calculation for the validation A/B.

Pass 4: Review pass — faion-sdd-executor-agent re-reads the requirement against constitution standards (no vanity metrics, has baseline + target + measurement method, validation plan defined). Humans own (a) the business question, (b) target setting (aggressive vs. realistic), (c) the go/no-go after the experiment.

antipattern.reason: Heavily-used features look high-priority because users who hated them already churned. Add churn-cohort comparison or you'll over-invest in features your remaining base loves and your lost base rejected.
"Page views" and "DAU" feel like business signal but are easy to game. Tie every requirement to a downstream conversion / retention / revenue metric, not a top-of-funnel proxy.

The "current value" cell ages out fast (seasonality, marketing campaigns, releases). Stamp every metric with as_of_date and a query/dashboard URL; reject baselines older than ~30 days for active surfaces.
A/B plans that don't pre-calculate MDE (minimum detectable effect) and required N produce "no significant difference" results that are actually underpowered. Use a power calculator before, not after.
Aggregate metrics improve while every segment gets worse (or vice versa). Always slice by acquisition channel, plan tier, geo, and device class before declaring a baseline.
PII or behavioral data may be unusable for product analytics under GDPR/CCPA without a lawful basis. Requirements built on data you cannot legally use will fail at the legal review gate.

"Users who use feature X retain 2x" almost never means "shipping feature X for everyone doubles retention". Self-selection swallows the effect. Frame requirements around hypotheses to be tested, not foregone conclusions.

95% of enterprise AI projects fail without a data strategy. The framework asks for the four impact areas — most teams fill three with guesses; mark unknowns as "to be measured" not as point estimates.

list.item: dbt — Version-controlled SQL transformations; agents reference dbt models as the canonical KPI definitions.
bq — BigQuery CLI; agents pull baselines as JSON, stamp as_of from query timestamp.
snowsql — Snowflake CLI equivalent for warehouse queries.
posthog (REST) — Self-hosted/SaaS product analytics with first-class API; pull funnels and cohort retention as evidence.
mixpanel (REST/JQL) — Event analytics queryable from agents; cohort + funnel endpoints.
metabase-cli / Metabase API — Open-source BI; agents render baseline charts and embed in the requirement doc.
pwr (R) / statsmodels (Python) — A/B power calculation; computes required N from baseline + MDE.
numpy + pandas + scipy.stats — Cohort segmentation, Simpson's-paradox checks, CI computation on baselines.
pandas-profiling / ydata-profiling — One-shot data quality report agents can attach to evidence section.
gh + jq — Pull product issue counts / churn signal from support repos as supplementary evidence.
claude CLI — Run the question / requirement / review passes against the JSON schema.
PostHog — OSS/SaaS, self-hostable; agents can run trends, funnels, cohort retention queries; ideal default for OSS-leaning teams.
Mixpanel — SaaS, strong for event-based KPI baselines; rate limits on free tier.
Amplitude — SaaS, cohort + retention + impact analysis exposed as API.
GA4 — SaaS (free), web-traffic baseline; agents fetch via google-analytics-data SDK.
Segment / Rudderstack — SaaS/OSS, source of truth for event schema; agents validate that proposed metrics are actually emitted.
Looker / Mode / Hex — SaaS, BI; agents reference saved Looks / models as canonical KPI definitions.
Metabase — OSS, cheap default BI for small teams; agents read Q&A endpoints.
Statsig / GrowthBook / Eppo / Optimizely — SaaS/OSS, A/B platforms; agents pre-register experiments with hypothesis, MDE, primary metric.
Hightouch / Census — SaaS, reverse-ETL; agents trigger sync of measured outcomes back into product DB once experiment ends.
Atlan / DataHub / OpenMetadata — SaaS/OSS, data catalogs; agents look up metric ownership, freshness SLA, lineage before citing a number.
Great Expectations / dbt tests — OSS, data-quality checks the agent can run before trusting a baseline.
Diverge on hypotheses before locking the question. Use brainstorm agents to generate multiple candidate hypotheses and KPI choices before committing to the analysis direction.

Gate-check requirements for rigor. Have sdd-executor-agent re-read every requirement: baseline cited, target numeric, measurement method named, validation plan present. Reject requirements with "TBD" in success metrics.
Use power calculation before running the test. Do not design A/B tests without pre-calculating MDE and required N; underpowered tests produce false negatives.
Timestamp every baseline. "Churn was 12%" is useless without "as of 2026-04-15 computed via SQL in dashboard Q.34". Require ISO dates and source URLs.
Triangulate with multiple data sources. One data source can be stale, biased, or wrong; use four independent signal types (usage + performance + business + feedback) to validate assumptions.
Redact PII before sharing baselines. Run Presidio on any sample row, any query result that contains email, ID, free text before pasting into Slack, Confluence, or an LLM prompt.

Measure quarterly calibration. After features ship, compare predicted target vs. measured outcome; log calibration error per author and per domain (e.g., "we systematically over-promise on conversion in payments").
Lock success criteria upfront. Define metric definitions and baseline queries in the requirement document before development; do not re-measure after you know the result.
Avoid vanity metrics. Tie every requirement to a downstream metric (revenue, retention, CSAT) rather than a top-of-funnel proxy (page views, DAU).

Require hypothesis framing. Every requirement must state what hypothesis is being tested and what outcome would constitute success vs. failure; avoid "improve the experience" without a specific, testable claim.

template.purpose: Requirement template with business context, data sources table, analysis summary, success metrics, and A/B validation plan.
